title: Offshore Support Vessel Operations (Saudi-flagged)
entry_mechanism: Saudi flagging programme through GAZT/GAQA combined with PSAP for vessel management capability
executive_summary:
Saudi Aramco operates one of the world's largest offshore oil and gas fields — requiring a fleet of offshore support vessels (OSVs) for anchor handling, platform supply, diving support, and marine construction. Today, a significant portion of this OSV fleet is foreign-flagged and foreign-operated. Node MR-2 targets the Saudisation of OSV operations: Saudi-flagged vessels, Saudi-crewed and Saudi-managed, operating in Saudi territorial waters.

The commercial logic is clear: Saudi Arabia's offshore activity generates $400–600 million annually in OSV charter fees, the majority flowing to foreign vessel owners and operators. A Saudi-flagged OSV fleet with Saudi crews retains this revenue in-Kingdom and creates 2,000–3,000 maritime jobs for Saudi nationals.

Saudi Arabia's Cabinet Resolution 100 (2020) mandated the Saudisation of offshore vessel operations. The challenge has been implementation — Saudi maritime human capital is insufficient to crew a large OSV fleet immediately, requiring a systematic training programme. Node MR-2 is the operational anchor for Saudi Arabia's maritime workforce development programme.

companies.name: Tidewater Marine
companies.rationale: Tidewater is the world's largest OSV operator and has active Saudi Arabia operations through Aramco contracts. A Saudi JV with Tidewater transfers operational management capability.

companies.name: BOURBON Maritime
companies.rationale: BOURBON operates OSVs globally and has established management partnerships in multiple emerging maritime nations.

companies.name: Siem Offshore
companies.rationale: Siem Offshore operates specialised OSVs including diving support and subsea vessels. Norwegian maritime expertise and active Gulf engagement.

companies.name: DOF Group
companies.rationale: DOF has subsea and OSV operations with a track record of establishing joint ventures in emerging maritime nations.

companies.name: Gulf Marine Services
companies.rationale: GMS is the Gulf's largest OSV operator and a regional benchmark. Saudi JV with GMS converts regional competitor to Saudi capability platform.

partners.name: Saudi Aramco Marine
partners.current: Some Saudi-flagged vessels; mostly foreign-chartered OSVs
partners.upgrade: Aramco 10-year Saudi OSV charter commitment; Saudi-flagged fleet expansion programme; Aramco technical supervision of Saudi crew training
partners.timeline: 24 months to first fully Saudi-crewed Saudi-flagged OSV

partners.name: Bahri Offshore
partners.current: VLCC and dry bulk operations; limited OSV capability
partners.upgrade: Bahri OSV division establishment; JV with Tidewater or BOURBON; Saudi fleet of 20 OSVs by Phase I end; Saudi maritime officer programme

partners.name: Saudi Maritime Academy (Rabigh)
partners.current: Maritime officer training; some OSV navigation training
partners.upgrade: OSV-specific maritime programme; DP (Dynamic Positioning) operator certification; offshore safety and survival training; 200 Saudi OSV officers per year
partners.timeline: 18 months to accredited OSV training programme

partners.name: Saudi Ports Authority
partners.current: Port operations; some OSV registration services
partners.upgrade: Saudi flag state administration expansion; OSV registration incentives; Saudi crew certification centre; streamlined flag registration for Saudi-owned OSVs
